subject: ap-chemistry
title: Solutions & Separation Techniques
stimulus:
A student investigates various dyes using paper chromatography. The student has samples of three pure dyes,
labeled A, B, and C, and an unknown sample that contains one of the three dyes. The student prepares the
chromatography chambers shown above on the left by putting a drop of each dye at the indicated position on
the chromatography paper (a polar material) and standing the paper in a nonpolar solvent. The developed
chromatograms are shown above on the right.

parts.prompt:
Which dye (A, B, or C) is the least polar? Justify your answer in terms of the interactions between the dyes
and the solvent or between the dyes and the paper.
parts.scoring_notes: Dye C is the least polar. In paper chromatography with a nonpolar solvent and polar paper, a less polar substance has stronger interactions with the nonpolar mobile phase and weaker interactions with the polar stationary phase (paper). Less polar dyes travel farther (higher R$_f$ value). From the chromatogram, Dye C travels farthest, indicating it is least polar.

scoring_points.description: For identifying Dye C as least polar with an interaction-based justification: 1 point

required_elements: Dye C is the least polar.
Least polar dye has stronger interactions with the nonpolar solvent and/or weaker interactions with the polar paper → travels farthest.
alternatives.correct_example: Dye C is the least polar because it moved the farthest up the chromatography paper. In paper chromatography, the stationary phase (paper) is polar and the mobile phase (solvent) is nonpolar. A nonpolar (least polar) dye has stronger London dispersion / nonpolar interactions with the nonpolar solvent and is least strongly attracted to the polar paper, so it travels the greatest distance.

wrong_examples: Saying Dye A is least polar — Dye A travels the shortest distance, meaning it is most strongly held by the polar paper (most polar).
Saying the most polar dye travels farthest — in a nonpolar solvent, less polar substances travel farther.
common_traps: In paper chromatography with a nonpolar solvent: least polar substance = farthest travel (most attracted to mobile phase, least attracted to stationary phase). Most polar = least travel.

parts.prompt:
Which dye is present in the unknown sample? Justify your answer.
2 C H OH(l) + 9 $O_2$(g) → 6 CO (g) + 8 $H_2O$(g)
3 7 2 2 2
parts.scoring_notes: Dye A is present in the unknown sample. Each dye produces a characteristic spot at a specific position (R$_f$ value) in the chromatogram. The spot from the unknown sample aligns with the spot from Dye A, indicating the same substance.

scoring_points.description: For identifying Dye A as the unknown with R$_f$ matching justification: 1 point

required_elements: Dye A is present in the unknown sample.
The unknown's spot travels the same distance as Dye A (same R$_f$), indicating they are the same substance.
alternatives.correct_example: Dye A is present in the unknown sample. The spot produced by the unknown sample is at the same position on the chromatogram as the spot for Dye A — they have the same R$_f$ value. Since each compound has a characteristic R$_f$ in a given solvent, this match identifies the unknown as Dye A.

wrong_examples: Saying Dye C — the unknown's spot does not travel as far as Dye C.
Saying Dye B — the unknown spot does not match the position of Dye B.
common_traps: R$_f$ = distance traveled by substance / distance traveled by solvent front. Two substances with the same R$_f$ under identical conditions are likely the same compound.

parts.prompt: The student is given a sample of an unknown substance and is told it could be one of the following: HCl(aq), NaCl(aq), or $C_6H_{12}O_6$(aq) (glucose). Which of these three substances is most likely to be the unknown if the measured conductivity of the solution is very high? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: HCl(aq) is the most likely substance. Electrical conductivity in solution depends on the presence of ions. HCl is a strong acid that dissociates completely into H$^+$(aq) and Cl$^-$(aq), producing a high concentration of mobile ions. NaCl also dissociates completely, but H$^+$ ions are more mobile than Na$^+$ ions. Glucose does not ionize at all. High conductivity most strongly suggests HCl.

scoring_points.description: For identifying HCl with ion concentration / strong electrolyte justification: 1 point

required_elements: HCl(aq) is the most likely substance.
HCl is a strong electrolyte that dissociates completely, producing a high concentration of mobile ions (including highly mobile H$^+$ ions) → very high conductivity.
alternatives.correct_example: HCl(aq). HCl is a strong acid that dissociates 100% into H$^+$(aq) and Cl$^-$(aq). Conductivity depends on the concentration and mobility of ions in solution. H$^+$ ions have exceptionally high mobility (proton hopping / Grotthuss mechanism), so HCl solutions are among the most conductive. NaCl also dissociates fully but Na$^+$ is less mobile than H$^+$. Glucose does not ionize at all and has near-zero conductivity.

wrong_examples: Saying glucose — glucose is a nonelectrolyte with essentially zero conductivity.
Saying NaCl — NaCl does conduct, but not as well as HCl because H$^+$ is far more mobile than Na$^+$.
common_traps: H$^+$ (proton) has exceptionally high molar conductance compared to other cations. For equal concentrations of HCl and NaCl, HCl will show significantly higher conductivity.

parts.prompt: A student is trying to separate a mixture of solid NaCl and solid $I_2$. Describe a procedure the student could use to separate the two solids. Include any reagents needed.
parts.scoring_notes: Add a nonpolar solvent (such as hexane or CCl$_4$) to the mixture. I$_2$ is nonpolar and dissolves in the nonpolar solvent while NaCl is ionic and does not dissolve in nonpolar solvents. Decant or pipette off the organic layer containing the dissolved I$_2$. Evaporate the solvent to recover solid I$_2$. NaCl remains as the undissolved residue.

scoring_points.description: For a correct separation procedure using differential solubility in a nonpolar solvent: 1 point

required_elements: Add a nonpolar solvent (e.g., hexane, CCl$_4$, or diethyl ether).
I$_2$ dissolves in the nonpolar solvent; NaCl does not.
Separate the layers / decant / filter to isolate NaCl; evaporate solvent to recover I$_2$.
alternatives.correct_example: Add a nonpolar solvent such as hexane to the mixture. I$_2$ is nonpolar and will dissolve in hexane; NaCl is ionic and will not dissolve. Decant or pipette the hexane layer (containing I$_2$) away from the solid NaCl. Evaporate the hexane to recover solid I$_2$. The NaCl remains behind.

wrong_examples: Adding water to dissolve both — NaCl dissolves in water but I$_2$ only slightly does; this would not cleanly separate them.
Using distillation without dissolving — distillation would require melting/sublimation at high temperature; extraction is simpler.
common_traps: Like dissolves like: I$_2$ (nonpolar) dissolves in nonpolar solvents; NaCl (ionic) dissolves in polar solvents like water. Choosing the right solvent exploits polarity differences.
